events.title: FOMC holds rates steady in March 2026, signals at most one cut for remainder of year
events.summary: The Federal Reserve held the federal funds rate unchanged at its March 2026 meeting. The dot plot and market-implied probabilities converged on a single cut at most for the rest of 2026, with a meaningful probability of no cut at all. This represents a hawkish shift from earlier market expectations of two cuts.
events.why_now: Markets had previously priced in roughly two cuts for 2026 (one around July, one by year-end). The FOMC's updated dot plot and Powell's commentary have pulled market expectations toward the Fed's own more restrictive guidance, reducing the likelihood of near-term easing.
events.market_relevance: Higher-for-longer rates compress equity valuations (especially growth/tech), support the dollar, weigh on rate-sensitive sectors (homebuilders, REITs, utilities), and keep mortgage rates elevated, directly impacting housing affordability and transaction volumes.

events.title: US economy unexpectedly lost 92,000 jobs in February; unemployment rose to 4.4%
events.summary: February 2026 non-farm payrolls showed a loss of 92,000 jobs versus an expected gain of 55,000 — a roughly 147,000 miss. The unemployment rate ticked up to 4.4%. This is a significant negative surprise suggesting the labor market may be cracking under the weight of sustained high rates and geopolitical disruption.
events.why_now: The data was released in early March and formed a key input to the FOMC's March deliberations. The combination of weakening employment and persistent inflation creates a stagflationary dynamic that constrains Fed policy options.
events.market_relevance: Rising unemployment threatens consumer spending (70% of GDP), increases credit delinquency risk for banks and consumer lenders, and could trigger defensive sector rotation. For real estate, weaker employment undermines housing demand and increases foreclosure risk, particularly in regions dependent on cyclical industries.

events.title: Core PCE inflation rose to 3.1% in January 2026, second consecutive monthly increase
events.summary: The Fed's preferred inflation gauge, core PCE, climbed to 3.1% in January — well above the 2% target and rising for a second straight month. This persistent inflation, even before the full pass-through of tariffs and the Iran conflict's energy price effects, severely limits the Fed's ability to cut rates to support the weakening labor market.
events.why_now: The January reading was confirmed ahead of the March FOMC meeting. With tariff effects still feeding through and energy prices spiking from the Middle East conflict, the inflation trajectory is likely to worsen before improving.
events.market_relevance: Persistent above-target inflation erodes real consumer purchasing power, pressures corporate margins (especially for companies unable to pass through costs), and keeps the risk-free discount rate elevated. For real estate, it sustains high mortgage rates and increases construction input costs.

events.title: Middle East conflict (war in Iran) driving energy prices higher, adding inflationary pressure
events.summary: An ongoing military conflict involving Iran is disrupting energy markets and pushing oil prices higher. The TreasurySpring analysis notes this as a key variable for the inflation outlook, with the size and duration of the energy price shock being the 'great unknown.' The conflict was referenced as a factor the FOMC must now look through when setting policy.
events.why_now: The conflict appears to have escalated in the weeks before the March FOMC meeting. Energy price spikes feed into transportation, manufacturing, and consumer costs with a lag of weeks to months, meaning the full inflationary impact has not yet materialized.
events.market_relevance: Energy price spikes benefit oil & gas producers and defense contractors while hurting airlines, transportation, chemicals, and consumer-facing businesses. Elevated energy costs act as a tax on consumers and businesses, potentially tipping a weakening economy into recession. For real estate, higher energy costs increase operating expenses for commercial properties and reduce household discretionary income available for housing.

events.title: Tariff effects have not yet fully passed through to consumer prices
events.summary: Analysis indicates that the full inflationary impact of previously imposed tariffs has not yet been reflected in price data. This means inflation may have remained well above the 2% target even without the Iran war's energy price effects, suggesting structural upward pressure on prices that the Fed cannot easily address with monetary policy alone.
events.why_now: With core PCE already at 3.1% and tariff pass-through still incomplete, the inflation outlook is deteriorating. This creates a policy trap: the Fed cannot cut rates to support employment without risking further inflation acceleration.
events.market_relevance: Companies reliant on imported inputs face continued margin pressure. Retailers and manufacturers with global supply chains are most exposed. The prospect of sustained tariff-driven inflation may force the Fed to maintain restrictive policy longer than markets expect, weighing on valuations broadly.

events.title: Fed Chair Powell pledges to stay; Warsh nomination blocked by Sen. Tillis — leadership uncertainty
events.summary: Fed Chair Jay Powell is pledging to remain in his position until a DoJ investigation concludes or is dismissed. Meanwhile, Senator Thom Tillis is attempting to block the Senate confirmation of Kevin Warsh, the President's nominee to replace Powell. Warsh has historically been hawkish but has recently adopted a more dovish stance aligned with the President's preference for rate cuts. The outcome of this leadership contest could significantly alter the Fed's policy direction.
events.why_now: The political maneuvering around the Fed Chair position is actively unfolding and creates binary risk for markets. A Powell-to-Warsh transition could signal a shift toward more accommodative policy, while Powell staying maintains the current data-dependent, cautious approach.
events.market_relevance: Fed leadership transitions create policy uncertainty that affects term premia in bonds, equity risk premiums, and dollar valuation. A more dovish successor could reprice the entire rate curve lower, benefiting rate-sensitive assets. Conversely, prolonged uncertainty itself is a headwind for risk assets.

events.title: Federal Reserve publishes 2026 bank stress test scenarios
events.summary: The Federal Reserve released the final 2026 stress test scenarios for large banks, with an effective balance sheet date of December 31, 2025. These scenarios define the hypothetical adverse conditions under which banks must demonstrate adequate capitalization. The severity of the scenarios will determine capital buffer requirements, which directly affect banks' ability to return capital to shareholders and extend credit.
events.why_now: The scenarios were finalized in early 2026 and results will be published later in the year. Banks are currently running their models against these scenarios, and the implied capital requirements will influence dividend and buyback decisions in the second half of 2026.
events.market_relevance: Stress test results determine bank capital return capacity (dividends, buybacks) and can trigger re-ratings of bank stocks. Stricter scenarios could constrain lending, affecting credit availability across the economy. For real estate, bank stress tests that include severe CRE loss scenarios could tighten commercial real estate lending standards.

events.title: Stagflation risk rising: simultaneous job losses, persistent inflation, and geopolitical energy shock
events.summary: The convergence of a negative jobs print (-92K), rising core PCE (3.1%), incomplete tariff pass-through, and an Iran-related energy price shock creates a textbook stagflationary environment. The Fed is effectively boxed in — unable to cut rates due to inflation, unable to tighten further without deepening labor market weakness. This macro regime is historically hostile to both equities and bonds simultaneously.
events.why_now: All of these factors crystallized in the weeks leading up to and including the March 2026 FOMC meeting, making this the first meeting where the full stagflationary picture was visible to policymakers and markets.
events.market_relevance: Stagflation historically favors real assets (commodities, energy, gold), inflation-protected securities, and companies with pricing power, while punishing long-duration bonds, growth equities, and leveraged balance sheets. For real estate, stagflation creates a mixed picture: nominal property values may hold due to inflation, but transaction volumes collapse as financing costs remain high and income growth stalls.
